sentences.text: Well, there-- there were these friends of mine who, when I returned from the latrine, pulled me down and said, stop creating any more problem than we already have.
We are waiting peacefully for something to happen.
And I said there is nobody at the watchtower.
And they didn't believe me.
And finally, one of the people had to go to execute his daily duties and he notices too.
And he conducted himself the same way as I did, by throwing a pebble at the watchtower.
And evidently, he hit the machine gun.
And the sound of this was like clinking [BACKROUND NOISE] in the air.
This is Alpine mountain, very cool mornings.
And he ran into barracks and he said, there is nobody on the watchtower.
There is no, there are no Germans around.
And then-- then people start getting out of the barracks and really trying to see if this is true, that the Germans had left as it was standing.
And sure enough, we got out from the-- Mathausen was having several small camps in one, in this huge camp.
So we got out from our barracks.
We got to the main appellplatz.

And what we witnessed was a American tank prying the gate of the-- huge gate there was, all usually open by two-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two group of people, for closing and opening, that was their duty, of the gate, was made out of locks.
And it was a very heavy and very well built.
Well, the American tank was prying it and succeeded to break through.
And then when I have seen the Americans walking, pouring in in the camp with the guns and looking at us, people were getting out, out of the way.
And finally, the tanks stopped, and American Red Cross army trucks are driving in masses.
And that's all I remember, because I fainted out of the sight that this is not the Red Star, but the White Star.
And I knew that they were Americans.
And I found myself in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a hospital down in [INAUDIBLE] of the former SS barracks that were converted to hospital.
And when the nurse came and I woke up, I-- I was saying to myself, well, was not so bad to die after all.
Look at this, there are chirping birds outside the windows.
You clean, nice clean bed.
And you are nobody to-- to bother you of getting up.
And then I suddenly realized that, what you talking about?
This is not dying.
You-- you are alive.
And then one of the American corps nurses came and asked me what language do I-- can converse with her.
And I said, well, either Polish or German.
And she said, very well, I'll be right back.
And she came with a nurse that spoke Polish and another one that was spoke German.
And they were conversing with me and said, well, you are doing all right.
You-- you-- you will be all right.
What would you like to have?
And I can remember just quite well that I asked for a large piece of bread with as much butter on it as the bread, thickness of the bread, with a glass of milk.
And I said, well, I said to myself, I can dream, can't I?
Whether I will get it is a different story.
And sure enough, in a few minutes she-- one of the nurses came with a tray.
And there was the bread with the butter, and marmalade, and things.
And I looked at her and I grabbed it.
Under the cover, I gulped it down faster than they-- than they would have possibly think that a human being can consume this thing.
And when I look from underneath the cover, there were three of them standing and crying.
Well, that [? turned me on. ?] I said, what's the matter with you?
What's going on?
Why-- why do you cry?
He said, we never believed that a human being can be so hungry.
We never were hungry in our life.
And I experienced some convulsions from it.
And I was complaining about it.
But somehow, by walking, and they help me for the first day to walk around and digest the food that I suddenly overwhelmingly consumed.
And after staying in the hospital for three days, I decided to leave without any permission, since I wanted to see all my friends in the camp.
When I got to the camp, they were still over there.
And there was registration going on for all the people who wanted to return to their homelands, or if they had relatives in other parts of the world, to go to other part of the world.
I know that I had an uncle in United States, brother of my father, who got to United States in 1905, when they wanted to, the Russians wanted to send him to the Japanese frontier.
And somehow, they managed to escape and come to the United States.
So--
I beg your pardon?
Samuel Frenkiel.
That's why changed my name to his, because it was sounding more English pronunciation.
They were pronounced Frenkiel there.
Well, the way I was writing in Poland.
And with me were the other members that we, that I mentioned from Skarzysko.
And there was Sam Bergman and Alek Ward, and Tsvi Karmi.
And at that time, his name was [?
Andrew ?] [?
Weinberg. ?] And Chaim Yitskowic, Moishe Rosenblum, and all of us, we register.
And two days later, they packed us up on army trucks with plenty of food and all kinds of rations.
And they made sure that we are well bathed and clean, with different clothing on.

And they brought us to the city of Regensburg to the American zone, because we wanted to migrate to other places and not to return to Poland, which we knew at that time was already under regime, Communist regime.
Very close, we became sort of a family that did not need any blood relation.
We still are writing and receiving in each other in a manner that perhaps some families could envy us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Of course, after the war, I did remember that I have a cousin in Israel who went to Israel in 1921 as a [INAUDIBLE].
And [BACKROUND NOISE] he became one of the builders of Kibbutz [INAUDIBLE], and became a doctor of [INAUDIBLE], and also renowned for other achievement in the agricultural life.
His son remain in [INAUDIBLE], born in [INAUDIBLE].
And I'm in constant contact with his two daughters and his son.
He passed away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in October of '91--
--at the age of 87.
His name was Dr.
Israel Slomnicki, spelled S-L-O-M-N-I-C-K-I.
Quite a few times.
I wanted and I wrote to him.
And since I was looking for my brother, it just happened that my brother from Belgium I was writing-- wrote a letter to him, and I, as I wrote from Germany.
And for out of sheer excitement-- this was in, during the siege of Jerusalem when him and his wife, [?
Leah-- ?] excuse me-- were teaching at the University of Jerusalem.
And out of sheer excitement, he could not write, but they reverse the letter, mine to my brother and my brother to me.
And added a note, do not come at the present time.
Do not come at the present time.

And when I met him quite a few years later, as a matter of fact, they it came to the United States to National Institute of Health in Bethesda, [INAUDIBLE] bases of the scientific, in scientific field.
She was a-- a doctor of biology, biochemistry.
And she was experimenting on a skin affected by radiation and becoming quite famous in this field.
During the time when there were some going on in Vietnam and there were fears of people being affected by radiation.
They [? came, ?] of course, every two weeks I was in Bethesda, Maryland.
I learn quite a bit about Washington, DC, and I was very happy about it.
There were many places that we spent together and we traveled together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While I came to United States, after--
When I came to United States in 1949, I came on the date of the Warsaw ghetto uprising of April 19, 1949.
I was liberated on May the 5th, 1945, the date of my mother's birth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was received quite warmly by my family.
And I felt like a-- a soldiers coming from war.
And they made my days quite memorable, that I was always grateful to them.
For whatever they needed, I responded in a way that one, I have to show the gratitude of being-- having such family.
And, he passed away and his wife passed away quite a long time ago.
But I'm in contact with their grandchildren, because both of the cousins that I had passed away.
They introduced me to such a good life in the United States, as seeing such famous show with [INAUDIBLE], South Pacific, and the other shows that are still living with me.
And I'm very proud of it.
There are very few people that perhaps can speak about the family, and the [? openness ?] and the [? friendliness, ?] and the way that we were received.
A month--
My brother remain in Belgium to finish his studies.
He survived as PO-- German POW, as a Polish POW in Germany in Stalag XI-A.
This camp was chosen by the Red Cross as a-- a-- a-- a pictures of a prisoner of the war, the Germans could not touch it, despite of the fact there were a number of Jews in there, among them.
And they were very, very careful about it.
And but fortunately, when the American frontier approach and reach the camp, many of these soldiers joined the US armed forces and fought alongside with them until the end of the war.
And then he returned back to Belgium, not realizing that perhaps I am survived.
But I had-- he had a feeling that I would somehow survive and be able to meet.
And after we exchanged the letters, he was on the way to convention in Prague, Czechoslovakia, for the gathering of textile engineers.
And we met unfortunately in-- [?in a?] most unexplainable place like Nuremberg in Germany.
But it was worthwhile.
Many of the people who realize that this is the first encounter between two brothers since 1937, and there was about 10 years.
It was 1947 since I met-- met him first time after the war.
And ever since then, we are in contact.

He finish his study, went to work-- to work in Canada, and finally worked for many industry, build many factories throughout the world, in Sa Long, in India, in Philippines, in Chile, in Brazil, in the Cuidad Trujillo.
At that time, there was Santo Domingo was called Cuidad Trujillo.
And he done quite a bit of his work in this field.
He was very, very proud of it.
He married-- he married in a Belgian girl, his first girlfriend from before the war, who kept all his things while he was in the army.
And they somehow are married very happy.
And we in constant contact with the one another.
He--
No, just the letters that I sent through a Polish friends.
They were send from Warsaw to his camp in-- in Germany.
At the beginning, we were allowed to send letters from the ghetto to-- to his camp.
And then we had to find contact on the Polish side to send letters to him.
Since this was a POW camp, Polacks were allowed to send letters to any place in Germany.
Did I-- did he?
No, I was never married.
And I discussed this with some of my friends.
And they were saying, whatever excuse I have is a very poor excuse to get-- not to get married.
Well, circumstances were such that I probably didn't fit into this marriage and the situation.
And perhaps at the late stages of my life, I'm regretting it more than anybody else.
Because I realize that to be alone is not just a-- a-- a simple thing of being alone, but you're missing something.
But according to the studies of the people, the whole construction of the of the marriage is to have children and have family.
And of course, the children supposed to take care of in the later stages of the parents.
This is not always the fact.
It's always that sometime that the parents, despite the fact that they are old and in need of the children, has to support the children.
Or they are so far away that they can't be of any help to them anyway.
So I don't know the controversial situation of being married.
However, I think that perhaps participating in this society, one should be married.
It would be of better social value.
I was in engineering.
The first job I had with a large breaking concern where machinery was involved, and I was working over there for 22 years.
And then I started to work for K-Mart apparel and with the sophisticated-- sophisticated equipment, where electronic equipment was involved.
And I had my educational background in a technical field from Germany and while I was waiting for my migration to the United States.
And in the United States attending various schools, among them Brooklyn Polytechnical.
The tragedy of the Holocaust there is, what people are talking about Holocaust they're, minimizing the true value of the word.
Holocaust is not just elimination and destruction of a certain race, group, or ethnic, or religious group.

But what happened is that perhaps among the six million, we lost, as a Jew, I can say that we lost more because we lost the children, the main factor that can reproduce and replace the values of Judaistic studies and Judaistic existence.

Not necessarily they have to be very religious or pay attention to this traditional life, but the base on this what we expect of the world is to deliver and to continue in good faith of delivering happiness among people in throughout the world, despite the fact that the world is still providing many ethnic groups and many people of destructive weapons and destructive forces for personal gains.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There are times where I recollect when I speak to youth groups and try to provide them the-- with the atrocities done to people as ethnic group, and not to permit the-- even for a moment, to separate or to point out that other ethnic groups, or races, or whatever it is of a different structure.
Every human being deserves just being a human being, deserves to be on this earth and experience a better condition to continue in the life.
And it is up to them to either to make it good or to lose the faith.

No, I'm fortunately not, I was fortunate enough that-- that right of liberation, after I [INAUDIBLE] left the hospital in [INAUDIBLE] and returned to the main camp in Mathausen, there was a Jewish doctor who encountered and was very much interested in finding youngsters of Jewish faith and talk to them.
And he mentioned to us that in case we having encountered any of the such conditions as self-blame, or that we survive and the others had to perish, is not the solution.
That we have to go on with life.

And through hard work and through a real devotion to other people problems of participating in the daily life, we perhaps can eliminate and go to bed tired to get a full day's, full night's sleep without being affected by any unnecessary dreams.
I read quite a few books on Holocaust, and the atrocities, and the experiences of other people.

And I was affected by it to a point that I did not understand, why didn't these people who were complaining about the nightmares and things like this, seek the professional advice to release them and provide them with a few more years of better life without suffering.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Out of the whole group that I know that we are in contact, these people are somehow living in fairly good condition, except one in Haifa that is fairly sick.
And he's constantly blaming himself that he couldn't find his brother, that he claim that he's alive.
And I was trying to help him through the-- belonging through the Holocaust Museum charter.
Being a charter member, I had the possibility of seeking information of certain individuals on their original name who arrive at a certain time in United States from various places.
And unfortunately, I could not have the heart to deliver to him the news that I couldn't find it.
And I was very, very upset about it.
But I had to also to realize that it is better to face the truth than go on and make false promises.
During my life, I encounter people on the basis of a straightforward approach.
And I was always happy when they responded in the most warmest way a individual could expect.
There's a certain group in Teaneck, New Jersey, that-- they are from the [?
Chabad ?] movement.
And I'm more than proud, they gave me so much comfort during the days when I needed, that I don't think that one should forget such people existence.
And the support, the moral support, and the actual-- actual support that they provided in-- in the moments that one felt that he is down on this earth.
I have to mention that last year I had the unfortunate encounter of being hit as, on a sidewalk by a car as a pedestrian.

And with the help of these people who were coming in [? trunks ?] to visit me in the hospital and bring me all kinds of goodies, I somehow got myself together that, to a point that I can just talk about it.
And like Jewish people say, this is a day of a miracle.

So I hope that this new Jewish year will provide us with a better outlook on, not only for us, but like they say Hebrew, [SPEAKING HEBREW], of all the Jewish nation of Israel, to encounter a better life and perhaps a peace that they are pray-- with we are praying so much about it, will finally come to the a point that people will live alongside of with one another and turn their weapons into plows of Joshua prediction for a better life upon this earth.
There are many atrocities in this world that still exist and perhaps under cover.
And there where the United Nation should really do something about it.
Unfortunately, it turned into a powerful money-collecting place, not paying sufficient amount of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things.
Let's not forget Holocaust and the children, the heroic children of Warsaw ghetto uprising, such people like [INAUDIBLE], Mordechai Ammilevitt, and the other leaders.
Their names should never be forgotten for the sake of better human relation in the world.
